critique.summary: The article describes Ghostling, a minimal terminal emulator demo built using the libghostty C API. It highlights Ghostling's features, its purpose as a demonstration of libghostty's capabilities, and clarifies that it is not intended as a full-featured terminal for daily use.
mainPoints: Ghostling is a minimal terminal emulator implemented in a single C file using libghostty and Raylib for rendering.
Libghostty is an embeddable library extracted from Ghostty's core, providing accurate and optimized terminal emulation with a C and Zig API.
Ghostling supports features like resize with text reflow, 24-bit color, Unicode handling, keyboard and mouse input, and various terminal protocols.
Ghostling is a demo and not a full-featured terminal; it lacks tabs, splits, session management, configuration UI, and search UI.
Libghostty is renderer-agnostic and can be used with any rendering system or language bindings beyond C and Zig.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with publicly available information about Ghostling and libghostty on GitHub. The description of features, design goals, and limitations matches the official repository documentation. There are no factual inaccuracies detected, and the technical details align with known terminal emulator implementations and libghostty's stated capabilities.
considerations: The article does not mention any performance benchmarks or comparisons with other terminal emulators.
There is no discussion of security implications or potential vulnerabilities in using libghostty or Ghostling.
The article does not address community adoption or the extent of real-world usage beyond the demo.
Potential challenges in integrating libghostty into other languages or environments are not explored in depth.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the use and development of Ghostty, a terminal emulator library, with users sharing their experiences integrating it into various projects and expressing interest in its features and potential. Several comments highlight its performance benefits, architectural considerations, and future possibilities for terminal emulation improvements.

comments.author: hombre_fatal
comments.text:
I built my own macOS terminal app over the last two weeks using swift/appkit + ghostty's zig library.

https://github.com/danneu/danterm

I basically just wanted vertical tabs and notifications for when AI agents (claude code, codex) are finished.

I already use it as my main terminal over iTerm.

It's a fun project since I use my terminal all day, so I always have ideas for how something could be improved or polished. When using someone else's software, like iTerm, I'd have to hope they fix it or pitch them on my UX ideals. When you roll your own, AI can do the annoying work of figuring out how to impl something, and you manage the last 10%.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a firsthand account of building a terminal app with Ghostty, offering practical insights and a link to the project, making it a reliable and constructive contribution.

comments.author: oDot
comments.text:
I use libghostty for Trolley[0], which packages TUIs as desktop apps, like Electron does for web apps.

It really is quite an amazing piece of software. I just wrapped it in a useful GUI and a bundle/package CLI and it just works. Even on Windows. Kudos to the Ghostty developers.

[0] https://github.com/weedonandscott/trolley
comments.analysis: The comment shares a positive experience using libghostty in a cross-platform project, reinforcing Ghostty's utility and stability.

comments.author: CraigJPerry
comments.text:
I used ghostty for the shell in my agent-manager tool (think something like https://air.dev/ but in SwiftUI. One architectural detail i'm still going back and forth on is: who should own the PTY?

If embedded Ghostty owns it, now i have to have some kind of workaround to instrument what's happening inside the terminal - signals, stdout/stderr events, return code etc.

If my parent agent-manager app owns it, now i don't have the nice clean small interface to ghostty (i'm a fan of John Ousterhout style narrow but deep interfaces, pulling complexity down rather than pushing up to parent).

Not sure if any other ghostty embedders might have advice. It's on my todo list to task a an agent to "gh repo clone" a few ghostty using shells and report on their arch.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced discussion about architectural design decisions when embedding Ghostty, reflecting deep technical understanding.

comments.author: lzhgusapp
comments.text: I switched to Ghostty a few months ago and it's become one of the apps I never close. The rendering speed is noticeably better than iTerm2, especially with large log outputs. Excited to see libghostty enabling projects like this — the idea of packaging TUIs as native desktop apps is really compelling for indie developers.
comments.analysis: A user testimonial highlighting performance improvements and enthusiasm for Ghostty, adding credibility to its benefits.

comments.author: vintagedave
comments.text:
The C file is small enough to read (over a few minutes.)

I got to about line 5 and realized: I’ve never seen quite that technique for embedding a font via an autogenerated header before. I’m more used to Windows resources; this seems to generate a byte array in CMake code. I’m somewhere between horrified and impressed, in that I feel we’ve finally discovered a cross platform binary resource embedding solution.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a technical observation about Ghostty's implementation details, showing familiarity with cross-platform resource embedding.

comments.author: thiht
comments.text: I really hope libghostty succeeds at setting a new baseline for what terminal emulation should be. Seems to be on a right track.
comments.analysis: An optimistic endorsement expressing hope for Ghostty's impact, though more subjective and less detailed.

comments.author: imiric
comments.text:
This looks interesting.

I don't need my terminal emulator to support tabs, windows, or session management. My WM manages tabs and windows, and I use tmux for sessions, which also gives me a scrollback buffer, selection, clipboard, search, etc. This combination allows me to use any simple terminal emulator, such as urxvt, st, and now foot, without issues.

Ghostty didn't appeal to me, but I might give this a try. It's good that OSC support is planned. A plugin-like system, similar to st's but less cumbersome, would be nice to have.
comments.analysis: A pragmatic viewpoint on terminal emulator features, showing a balanced perspective and openness to testing Ghostty despite initial reservations.

comments.author: lindskogen
comments.text:
I have an idea of a terminal emulator where you could maximize panes but using a nested structure, does anyone know of one?

Standard "Zoom" features in tmux or iTerm2 only maximize the single active pane to the full window, hiding everything else. If I have a layout like this:

  _____________________
  |         |    B    |
  |    A    |---------|
  |         |    C    |
  |_________|_________|
And I expand B, I want A to hide, while B and C remain visible together. Then I can create a new nested workspace in there and later zoom out when I’m done.

Maybe this could be done arbitrarily deep?
comments.analysis: This comment proposes an innovative UI concept for terminal multiplexers, contributing creatively to the discussion.

comments.author: znpy
comments.text:
I’m seriously interested in this. I wonder if i can use this along some decent gui library and an llm to vibe-code a SecureCRT replacement.

SecureCRT is awesome but it’s crazy expensive :(
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and a potential use case combining Ghostty with AI, though speculative.

comments.author: delduca
comments.text: I have plans to add it to my game (SDL).
comments.analysis: A brief statement of intent to integrate Ghostty, showing interest but lacking detail.
